learning_outcomes.description: Understand better the complex nature of Australian society, its different elements, and their separate and shared pasts.
Reflect critically on Australian history and its impacts on the present and the future.
Demonstrate reasoned thought, creativity, and a high quality of written expression.
Examine, discuss and debate the main issues regarding Australian history, how it is understood today, and its importance to the present and the future.
Demonstrate respect for and understanding of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples and perspectives.

assessments.category: Participation/ Student contribution, Tutorial/ Problem Set
assessments.task_description:
PARTICIPATION AND ATTENDANCE (10%)

Students should come prepared to participate in the weekly class and small-group discussions. Your contribution to tutorials is expected to be an informed one, and will be worth up to ten (10) percent of your final grade. Unexplained absences will result in a one (1) mark deduction per tutorial missed. 

Students should prepare properly for each class, and their contribution should indicate they have given some thought to the issues under consideration. Students who are vociferous each week but whose participation is based upon their general knowledge rather than careful reading of the documents and texts cannot expect to receive a high mark.

Tutorials are meant to be inclusive, interactive meetings: students who are content to do nothing in tutorials except note down what other students, or the tutor, are saying, cannot expect to receive a good mark for tutorial participation.

You are expected to be on time for tutorials, and to stay for the entire class.

If you are unable to attend, please notify your tutor at the earliest possible opportunity. Make-up assessment of a one page response to the discussion questions may be possible.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
An essay outline and Bibliography of no less than 500 words for the Major Essay should be submitted to turnitin by 2pm on Friday of Week 5. 

Consult the Major Essay summary for further information. 

In this exercise, you should address the following points: 

  • Identify your question (write it out!).
  • Outline the importance/significance of the topic in understanding the Australian past.
  • Give a concise statement of your argument (though this may change as you progress).
  • Outline your research approach, strategy and time plan.
  • Identify and explain your main sources. You must include three key primary sources and four key secondary sources (which must be academic books, book chapters or articles). Explain why they are valuable and how you intend to use them. Choose carefully as these must also be the key sources for your major essay.
  • Provide a bibliography of ten (including the key sources listed above) items for you to use in your essay, and where you will find them (eg, UQ Library, National Archives of Australia online).

A word count that is within ±10% of the set length (word limit) is acceptable. Word count does not include footnotes; however, discursive footnotes are included in the word count. A word count that is outside these 10% will receive a proportionate penalty and will be graded against the grading criteria.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

If you use AI or MT to assist with this essay, you must include a paragraph stating how you used it, whether you found it useful, and why or why not. This paragraph will not contribute to the word count for the assignment.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

Referencing Systems

  • The Essay Outline assignment may include footnotes, though this is not necessary.
  • All Essay Outlines must include a bibliography. 
  • The required referencing system used is Chicago 18A, guides for which are available on the UQ Library website. 
  • Your footnotes and bibliography are not counted in the word length. Please do not use discursive footnotes in this exercise, or in-text references.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit through the Turnitin portal on the course Blackboard page.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
The major essay requires students to write an essay on the basis of primary and secondary historical research.

The essay is 2000 words excluding bibliography and footnotes. 

Please select one of the essay questions below.

  1. In what ways did convict women challenge and disrupt the systems that were intended to control them?
  2. Some convicts thrived in Australia. What was it about the penal colonies that made them places of opportunity for convicts, as well as places of punishment, at least until the 1820s?
  3. What philosophies, and what practical reasons, led to such negative perceptions of Indigenous Australians in the colonial period?
  4. Is the violence that developed between British colonists and Aboriginal people on the Australian colonial frontier best described as a case of ‘war’ or was it made up of a series of individual acts? What are the implications of each interpretation?
  5. Describe attitudes towards the Chinese on the gold fields in the 1850s. Why were the Chinese seen as such a threat?
  6. To what extent does the Anzac Legend simplify understandings of the Australian First World War soldiering experience? In your response you may like to consider the composition of the First AIF, the different battlefields on which Australians served, and soldiers’ varied reactions to war.
  7. What happened to the children, families and communities of the Stolen Generations? How was the removal of Aboriginal children from their families justified by the government and white society?
  8. Some historians have viewed WWII as a turning point for women in Australia, while others have seen it as having little long term impact. How did women's lives change in WWII, and were there any lasting impacts?
  9. In what ways did Australia use international diplomacy to try and improve its security in the decade after World War II?
  10. After the Second World War, the Australian government encouraged mass European immigration, reassuring current Australian residents that the newcomers would have to assimilate to the Australian way of life. What is assimilation and to what extent did this policy affect the lives of “New Australians”? 
  11. How successful were the "liberationist" campaigns of the 1960s and early 1970s? Discuss with reference to one campaign (e.g. feminism, gay rights, sexual liberation, Black Power etc).

A word count that is within ±10% of the set length (word limit) is acceptable. Word count does not include footnotes; however, discursive footnotes are included in the word count. A word count that is outside these 10% will receive a proportionate penalty and will be graded against the grading criteria.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

If you use AI or MT to assist with this essay, you must include a paragraph stating how you used it, whether you found it useful, and why or why not. This paragraph will not contribute to the word count for the assignment.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

Referencing Systems

  • The essay assignment must be properly referenced.
  • All essays must include a bibliography. 
  • The required referencing system used is Chicago 18A, guides for which are available on the UQ Library website. 
  • Your footnotes and bibliography are not counted in the word length. Please do not use discursive footnotes in this exercise, or in-text references.

assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
The purpose of the exam is to identify the level of basic knowledge you have acquired and retained from the course overall, and your grasp of the broader historical themes and issues. It will cover lecture content and tutorial discussions and readings.

The exam will require you to complete four short essays of approximately 250 words each within the allotted two-hour time frame. There will be ten questions in total.

One question will be compulsory. This is a general question addressing one of the central themes of the course.

The other nine questions will be more specific. You will be required to answer three of these.

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.topic:
Introduction

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Introduction

learning_activities.topic:
Aboriginal Australia and British "Discovery"

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Aboriginal Australia and British "Discovery"

learning_activities.topic:
First Contact

This week, we will explore those seismic moments of first contact between Europeans and Indigenous Australians. The Europeans brought with them significant “cultural baggage” (ideas about what Indigenous people would be like, how they would interact, and what their society might mean). This was based on hundreds of years of exploration across the globe. These stories of travel and adventure would be told and retold for receptive European audiences, building a highly selective picture of Indigenous life and colonised lands. In particular, the Europeans had a strong belief in their own racial and cultural superiority. Aboriginal people also had a long interaction with other cultures, particularly those from areas in Asia and the Pacific. They had, over thousands of years, developed relationships with these visitors, who came, traded, and left. This too impacted on early meetings between Aboriginal people and the Europeans.

In these readings, we will consider the initial reactions of both Europeans and Aboriginal peoples to the invasion of Indigenous land, and those earliest moments of contact between the two disparate cultures. Of course, this is quite difficult, as our records are invariably one-sided, with a strong written narrative from the Europeans, and an oral culture from Indigenous Australians. Here, we will read “against the grain”, and attempt to understand Indigenous perspectives through later oral histories, which have proven remarkably reliable sources. The two primary sources provide an important contrast—Tench is an educated, sophisticated career soldier who arrived with the First Fleet, and is well versed in Enlightenment theories of race and colonialism. Hobbles Daniyeri, in contrast, is a tribal elder in the Northern Territory, where this oral history was recorded in 1982 by a white anthropologist, Deborah Bird Rose. His history offers a rich and stimulating view of Indigenous opinions of the colonisation process, using Captain Cook as the “everyman” invader. The third source is from Inga Clendinnen’s Boyer Lectures, originally broadcast on the ABC in 1998. 

We will consider the multiple ways Indigenous people reacted to the colonisers: capitulation, resistance, accommodation. We will consider also how the Europeans viewed the Eora people, including what they admired, and what they viewed as “savage”.

Readings:

1) Tench, Watkin. Watkin Tench’s 1788. Edited and Introduced by Tim Flannery. Melbourne: Text, 2012, pp. 78-89.

2) Rose, Deborah Bird. “The saga of Captain Cook: Morality in European and Aboriginal Law", Aboriginal Studies, no. 2, 1984, pp. 31-2.

3) Clendinnen, Inga. True Stories: History, Politics, Aboriginality. Melbourne: Text, 2008, pp. 5-23.

Discussion Questions:

How does Watkin Tench describe the Eora people? What traditions does he draw upon? How does Hobbles Daniyeri describe the colonisation process? What traditions does he draw upon? In what ways does Clendinnen suggest that we use an appreciation of these different perspectives on contact to inform our understanding of Australian history more generally?


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05, L06

Melbourne: Text, 2012, pp. 78-89. 2) Rose, Deborah Bird. “The saga of Captain Cook: Morality in European and Aboriginal Law", Aboriginal Studies , no. 2, 1984, pp. 31-2. 3) Clendinnen, Inga. learning_activities.topic:
From Convicts to the Gold Rushes

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: From Convicts to the Gold Rushes

learning_activities.topic:
Convict Women

Prior to the 1960s, convicts were largely regarded as the refuse of the civilised world, expelled from Britain due their hideous, unreformable criminality. Convict women were regarded almost universally as “whores”. Prominent Australian historian AGL Shaw wrote in his 1966 book Convicts and the Colonies that “Of the females less need be said, for they represented only about one sixth of the total… the picture they presented is a singularly unattractive one.” He also suggested that they “were not the sort of women to attract men into marriage.”

In this tutorial we will consider how and why historical thinking about convicts generally, and convict women in particular, has changed since Shaw’s time.

Readings:

1) Summers, Anne. Damned Whores and God’s Police: The Colonization of Women in Australia. Penguin: Melbourne, 1975, 313-35.

2) Oxley, Deborah. “Packing her (Economic) Bags: Convict Women Workers.” Australian Historical Studies 26 (1994): 57-76.

3) McCabe, Kristine. “Assignment of Female Convicts on the Hunter River, 1831-1840.” Australian Historical Studies 30 (1999): 286-302.

Discussion Questions:

What assumptions are embedded in Shaw’s comments above? How do Summers, Oxley and McCabe undermine the ideas of useless and powerless convict women bereft of morality? In what ways do these three articles represent a feminist history challenge to previous understandings of the convict experience?

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Convict Women Prior to the 1960s, convicts were largely regarded as the refuse of the civilised world, expelled from Britain due their hideous, unreformable criminality. Convict women were regarded almost universally as “whores”. Prominent Australian historian AGL Shaw wrote in his 1966 book Convicts and the Colonies that “Of the females less need be said, for they represented only about one sixth of the total… the picture they presented is a singularly unattractive one.” He also suggested that they “were not the sort of women to attract men into marriage.” In this tutorial we will consider how and why historical thinking about convicts generally, and convict women in particular, has changed since Shaw’s time. Readings: 1) Summers, Anne. Damned Whores and God’s Police: The Colonization of Women in Australia . Penguin: Melbourne, 1975, 313-35. 2) Oxley, Deborah. “Packing her (Economic) Bags: Convict Women Workers.” Australian Historical Studies 26 (1994): 57-76. 3) McCabe, Kristine. “Assignment of Female Convicts on the Hunter River, 1831-1840.” Australian Historical Studies 30 (1999): 286-302. Discussion Questions: What assumptions are embedded in Shaw’s comments above? How do Summers, Oxley and McCabe undermine the ideas of useless and powerless convict women bereft of morality? In what ways do these three articles represent a feminist history challenge to previous understandings of the convict experience?

learning_activities.topic:
Making Modern Australia

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Making Modern Australia

learning_activities.topic:
Frontier Violence

It was once accepted that Australia was peacefully settled, that Europeans moved into an empty land and no-one seriously opposed them. In recent times, however, many historians have pointed out that Aboriginal people owned Australia before the arrival of the Europeans, and that they fiercely resisted European encroachment onto their traditional lands which provided their livelihood and with which they had an intimate spiritual connection. The new interpretation of Australian history – which has become mainstreamed now – has used terms such as “invasion” and “genocide,” both of which have seriously challenged many people’s traditional understandings of, and connections with, Australian history.

In this tutorial we examine some of the causes and forms of frontier conflict and acknowledge that the fear and killing was not entirely one-sided. We also consider a chapter by Henry Reynolds, one of the foremost authors of colonial race relations, and his thoughts on why many non-indigenous Australians find the revelation of frontier violence so unsettling. Reynolds wrote this chapter some twenty-five years ago when the public debate over frontier killings was reaching a crescendo, but it is still illuminating and relevant today. I acknowledge that here is a lot of reading for this week, but I think you’ll find it worth it.

Readings:

1) Boyce, James. Van Diemen’s Land. Melbourne: Black Inc., 2009, pp. 186-209.

2) Clements, Nicholas, and Andrew Gregg. “’I am Frightened out of My Life’: Black War, White Fear,” Settler Colonial Studies 7 (2) (2017): 221-40.

3) Bottoms, Timothy. Conspiracy of Silence: Queensland’s Frontier Killing Times. Sydney: Allen and Unwin, 2013, pp. 12-30.

4) Reynolds, Henry. Why Weren’t We Told?: A Personal Search for the Truth About Our History. Melbourne: Viking, 1999, 117-133.

Discussion Questions:

What drove the frontier violence in Tasmania? In SE Queensland? What were the aims of the contending parties? What did they stand to lose? On the evidence presented, what are the differences between Aboriginal and European uses of violence? In terms of the History Wars, why is it so hard to ‘prove’ massacres of Aboriginal people? Are arguments over the extent of frontier violence a debate over the ways historians use evidence or is it a debate about how we wish to view Australia’s colonial past and present?

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05, L06

learning_activities.topic:
Nationalism, Federation and White Australia

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Nationalism, Federation and White Australia

learning_activities.topic:
Australia & WWI

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Australia & WWI

learning_activities.topic:
The Anzac Legend

Anzac is one of the pillars of Australian nationalism and national identity, and has been ever since the 1920s. It is the closest thing we have to a sacred national institution. Since the 1990s, increasing numbers of Australians express their connection to Anzac by participating in Anzac-related events, including – particularly for young people – embarking on a ‘pilgrimage’ to Gallipoli. But the Anzac legend has always been based on a selective reading of realities. It includes and exaggerates much that is admirable about the Anzacs and their deeds, and leaves out most of the less admirable, even damning, features of their experiences. It is a process of sanctification.

In this tutorial we will explore what it is about the experience of the Australian soldiers at Gallipoli that has seen this historical event elevated above all other national events and stories to embody Australian values and character, and discuss how and why more Australians – over 100 years after Gallipoli – recognise Anzac Day as the most important national day.

Readings:

1) Ashmead-Bartlett, Ellis, “Australians at the Dardanelles: Thrilling Deeds of Heroism,” The Argus, 8 May 1915. In Select Documents in Australian History: Volume I – 1901- 1939, ed. CMH Clark. Sydney: Angus and Robertson, 1955, pp. 234-236.

2) Beaumont, Joan. “The Anzac Legend.” In Joan Beaumont (ed), Australia’s War 1914-18. Sydney: Allen & Unwin, 1995, pp. 149-80.

3) McKenna, Mark. “Anzac Day: How did it become Australia’s national day?” in What’s Wrong With Anzac? The Militarisation of Australian History, eds. Marilyn Lake, Henry Reynolds, Mark McKenna and Joy Damousi. Sydney: UNSW Press, 2010, pp.110-134.

Discussion Questions:

How does Ashmead-Bartlett describe the Australian soldiers? How do you think his report was received by Australians at home? Why, as Joan Beaumont asks, did the legend acquire such force and persuasiveness? How does McKenna explain the evolution of Anzac Day to become Australia’s ‘de facto’ national day? Why are history and memory different? What significance does this hold for the way we remember the Gallipoli campaign (and the First World War in general) in Australia?

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05, L06

learning_activities.topic:
Division & Depression

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Division & Depression

learning_activities.topic:
A broken nation?

Historian Joan Beaumont has argued that the First World War left Australia a ‘broken nation’. The deep political, religious and class divides of the war years continued in its aftermath. Families and communities struggled to deal with the loss of tens of thousands of Australian men killed on the battlefields of Gallipoli, the Western Front, and in the Middle East. They—and the nation more broadly—also had to navigate the unprecedented political, economic and emotional challenges of supporting many thousands more veterans as they attempted to rebuild their lives in the postwar period.

Further hardship was to come. Global economic downturns in the 1920s led to economic disaster and political turmoil in Australia and, by the 1930s, Australians were facing yet another crisis—what came to be known as the Great Depression.

In this tutorial we will consider the reverberations of the Depression years in Australia, and the extent to which it was a time of social misery for all Australians. We will also explore how different historians have approached this era, and consider why some work has generated such controversy and debate.  

Readings:

1) Potts, David. “A Positive Culture of Poverty Represented in Memories of the 1930s Depression. Journal of Australian Studies 14 (1990): 3-14.

2) Scott, Joanne, and Saunders, Kay. “Happy Days are Here Again? A Reply to David Potts.” Journal of Australian Studies 17 (1993): 10-22.

3) Beaumont, Joan. “The Returned Soldier as a Site of Memory: Employment Preference and War Pensions during the Great Depression in Australia.” Australian Historical Studies 52 (2021): 8-26.

Discussion Questions:

What factors determined how people experienced the 1930s in Australia? Aside from its social impacts, what were some of the more emotional effects of the Depression on those who lived through it? Why were returned soldiers seen to be a privileged group during the Depression? Were they? What do you think of Potts’ approach? Why have other historians critiqued his analysis? What does this debate reveal about broader historiographical trends and tensions in Australian History? 


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05, L06

learning_activities.topic:
WWII & the Remaking of Australia

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: WWII & the Remaking of Australia

learning_activities.topic:
Encountering Asia

World War II was a bitter experience for many Australians, particularly on account of the fear of Japanese invasion and the miseries suffered by the 22,000 Australians taken POW by the Japanese, of whom some 8,000 died. The war is usually seen as confirming Australian fears of Asia, fears which were exacerbated by the fall of China to the communists in 1949 and the potential spread of communist aggression to Australia’s doorstep, hence our involvement in Korea, Malaya and Vietnam. But as Asian economies expanded rapidly from the 1970s onwards, and as Britain entered what was then the European Economic Community, Australia is popularly conceived of as having successfully rediscovered Asia. As these readings indicate, though, many Australians had encountered and been engaging with Asia, in various ways, during the war and in the years after.

Readings:

1) Grant, Lachlan. “Good neighbours’ or ‘police dogs of imperialism’: attitudes to empire” in Australian Soldiers in Asia-Pacific during World War II. Sydney: NewSouth, 2014.

2) Twomey, Christina. “Peace in our region: prisoners of war and Australia’s relationship with Japan 1945-1960.” In Yasuko Claremont (ed), Civil Society and Postwar Pacific Basin Reconciliation: Wounds, Scars and Healing. Abingdon: Routledge, 2018, pp. 59-73.

3) Sobocinska, Agnieszka. “Good Neighbours” in Visiting the Neighbours: Australians in Asia. Sydney: NewSouth, 2014.

Discussion Questions:

How did the war in the Pacific affect Australian soldiers' attitudes towards Asia? How does Grant account for differences expressed by soldiers towards Australia's future role in the region? Why did many former POWs – who were, as Twomey states, often perceived to be the leaders of anti-Japanese sentiment in Australia – promote peace and reconciliation with Japan? How did personal travel, and the published writings of travellers, shape Australian understandings of the Asian region in the decades after the war?

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Encountering Asia World War II was a bitter experience for many Australians, particularly on account of the fear of Japanese invasion and the miseries suffered by the 22,000 Australians taken POW by the Japanese, of whom some 8,000 died. The war is usually seen as confirming Australian fears of Asia, fears which were exacerbated by the fall of China to the communists in 1949 and the potential spread of communist aggression to Australia’s doorstep, hence our involvement in Korea, Malaya and Vietnam. But as Asian economies expanded rapidly from the 1970s onwards, and as Britain entered what was then the European Economic Community, Australia is popularly conceived of as having successfully rediscovered Asia. As these readings indicate, though, many Australians had encountered and been engaging with Asia, in various ways, during the war and in the years after. Readings: 1) Grant, Lachlan. “Good neighbours’ or ‘police dogs of imperialism’: attitudes to empire” in Australian Soldiers in Asia-Pacific during World War II. Sydney: NewSouth, 2014. 2) Twomey, Christina. “Peace in our region: prisoners of war and Australia’s relationship with Japan 1945-1960.” In Yasuko Claremont (ed), Civil Society and Postwar Pacific Basin Reconciliation: Wounds, Scars and Healing. Abingdon: Routledge, 2018, pp. 59-73. 3) Sobocinska, Agnieszka. “Good Neighbours” in Visiting the Neighbours: Australians in Asia. Sydney: NewSouth, 2014. Discussion Questions: How did the war in the Pacific affect Australian soldiers' attitudes towards Asia? How does Grant account for differences expressed by soldiers towards Australia's future role in the region? Why did many former POWs – who were, as Twomey states, often perceived to be the leaders of anti-Japanese sentiment in Australia – promote peace and reconciliation with Japan? How did personal travel, and the published writings of travellers, shape Australian understandings of the Asian region in the decades after the war?

learning_activities.topic:
Migration & Assimilation

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Migration & Assimilation

learning_activities.topic:
The Stolen Generations

In April 1997, the Human Rights and Equal Opportunity Commission released Bringing Them Home: Report of the National Inquiry into the Separation of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Children from their Families. The long-awaited report very clearly illustrated what Indigenous families had known for a long time: that children had been wilfully separated from families to help to bring about their assimilation, education and employment. These were not children who had been abused: instead, children were removed on the grounds of their colour and race. ‘Mixed race’ children were particularly removed, as it was believed they could most readily be assimilated into white society as workers. This devastating policy—based on ideas of racial hierarchies—had long-term, continuing impacts on Indigenous societies, as generations of families were splintered. 

The Report brought together a wealth of evidence about the history of child removal; indeed, one of the most significant things about it is the oral evidence it presents, some of which you are reading this week. However, while many Australians welcomed the Report, it was also widely criticised for a flawed methodology, an overly partisan viewpoint, and especially for the finding that Aboriginal child-removal policies and practices amounted to genocide. 

One of the surprising things to come from the story of the stolen generations is the common response of non-indigenous Australians who profess shock and outrage over these practices, insisting they had little awareness of them at the time.

Readings:

1) Human Rights and Equal Opportunity Commission, Bringing Them Home: Report of the National Inquiry into the Separation of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Children from their Families, Human Rights and Equal Opportunity Commission, Sydney, 1997, Chapter 10: Children’s Experiences.

2) Haebich, Anna, “‘Between Knowing and not Knowing’: Public Knowledge of the Stolen Generations”, Aboriginal History, vol. 25, 2001, pp. 70-90.

Discussion Questions:

What does the Bringing Them Home report tell us about the experience of child removal? What were some of the consequences for Indigenous children and their families? Thinking as a historian, what are some of the advantages and potential issues of testimony such as that provided in the Bringing Them Home Report? Are personal histories more useful for telling histories of oppression than ‘big picture’ histories? Do they help us to understand the impact of the policies of child removal any better? How could a racially based policy of child removal have persisted in Australia for so long? How much did people really know about what was happening? And why might knowing or not knowing, as Anna Haebich puts it in the readings for this week, matter to us as historians?

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05, L06
learning_activities.topic_description: The Stolen Generations In April 1997, the Human Rights and Equal Opportunity Commission released Bringing Them Home: Report of the National Inquiry into the Separation of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Children from their Families . The long-awaited report very clearly illustrated what Indigenous families had known for a long time: that children had been wilfully separated from families to help to bring about their assimilation, education and employment. These were not children who had been abused: instead, children were removed on the grounds of their colour and race. ‘Mixed race’ children were particularly removed, as it was believed they could most readily be assimilated into white society as workers. This devastating policy—based on ideas o f racial hierarchies—had long-term, continuing impacts on Indigenous societies, as generations of families were splintered. The Report brought together a wealth of evidence about the history of child removal; indeed, one of the most significant things about it is the oral evidence it presents, some of which you are reading this week. However, while many Australians welcomed the Report, it was also widely criticised for a flawed methodology, an overly partisan viewpoint, and especially for the finding that Aboriginal child-removal policies and practices amounted to genocide. One of the surprising things to come from the story of the stolen generations is the common response of non-indigenous Australians who profess shock and outrage over these practices, insisting they had little awareness of them at the time. Discussion Questions: What does the Bringing Them Home report tell us about the experience of child removal? What were some of the consequences for Indigenous children and their families? Thinking as a historian, what are some of the advantages and potential issues of testimony such as that provided in the Bringing Them Home Report? Are personal histories more useful for telling histories of oppression than ‘big picture’ histories? Do they help us to understand the impact of the policies of child removal any better? How could a racially based policy of child removal have persisted in Australia for so long? How much did people really know about what was happening? And why might knowing or not knowing, as Anna Haebich puts it in the readings for this week, matter to us as historians?

learning_activities.topic:
Essay Writing Workshop

This week is dedicated to an interactive discussion about essay writing, a vital academic practice for students of all disciplines. We will work through the processes of constructing a good essay, and some of the typical pitfalls. There is no required reading for this tutorial, but please come with questions in mind.


Learning outcomes: L03, L04

We will work through the processes of constructing a good essay, and some of the typical pitfalls. There is no required reading for this tutorial, but please come with questions in mind.

learning_activities.topic:
The '60s, '70s, & the New Left / Indigenous Protest

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: The '60s, '70s, & the New Left / Indigenous Protest

learning_activities.topic:
Sexual LIberation & its Limits

The 1960s and 1970s saw remarkable changes in public life, but also significant change in the most private of realms—the bedroom. Sexual morality loosened, and traditional and conservative views on sex lost a lot of their influence. These changes were, however, uneven for different groups of Australians, came in stages, and were often less than complete. One of the key features of this period was the uptake of the contraceptive pill, examined closely by Frank Bongiorno. 

For some groups, however, sexuality was restricted by far more than the fear of pregnancy. In the second of the readings for this week, Graham Willett takes us into the early activism of the gay liberation movement, examining the period between the bold Gay Pride activities of 1973 and the first Mardi Gras of 1978.

Readings:

1) Bongiorno, Frank. "January 1961 - The Release of the Pill: Contraceptive Technology and the 'Sexual Revolution'". In Martin Crotty and David A. Roberts (eds), Turning Points in Australian History. Sydney: UNSW Press, 2008, pp. 157-70.

2) Willett, Graham. Living Out Loud: A History of Gay and Lesbian Activism in Australia. Sydney: Allen and Unwin, 2000, pp. 108-27. 

Discussion Questions:

What was so remarkable about the pill as a reproductive technology? What were its implications for different groups of Australians? What were the barriers to its adoption by women? What does Bongiorno see as its most significant effects? Was the widespread adoption of the pill a cause of the sexual revolution, or a symptom of it? Was the pill at all connected to gay liberation? If so, how? Why did the gay liberation movement change its tactics in this period, and did quiet activism achieve anything of substance? Had sexual liberation become a reality by the end of the 1970s?

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Sexual LIberation & its Limits The 1960s and 1970s saw remarkable changes in public life, but also significant change in the most private of realms—the bedroom. Sexual morality loosened, and traditional and conservative views on sex lost a lot of their influence. These changes were, however, uneven for different groups of Australians, came in stages, and were often less than complete. One of the key features of this period was the uptake of the contraceptive pill, examined closely by Frank Bongiorno. For some groups, however, sexuality was restricted by far more than the fear of pregnancy. In the second of the readings for this week, Graham Willett takes us into the early activism of the gay liberation movement, examining the period between the bold Gay Pride activities of 1973 and the first Mardi Gras of 1978. Readings: 1) Bongiorno, Frank. "January 1961 - The Release of the Pill: Contraceptive Technology and the 'Sexual Revolution'". In Martin Crotty and David A. Roberts (eds), Turning Points in Australian History . Sydney: UNSW Press, 2008, pp. 157-70. 2) Willett, Graham. Living Out Loud: A History of Gay and Lesbian Activism in Australia. Sydney: Allen and Unwin, 2000, pp. 108-27. Discussion Questions: What was so remarkable about the pill as a reproductive technology? What were its implications for different groups of Australians? What were the barriers to its adoption by women? What does Bongiorno see as its most significant effects? Was the widespread adoption of the pill a cause of the sexual revolution, or a symptom of it? Was the pill at all connected to gay liberation? If so, how? Why did the gay liberation movement change its tactics in this period, and did quiet activism achieve anything of substance? Had sexual liberation become a reality by the end of the 1970s?

learning_activities.topic:
Reflection & Revision

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Reflection & Revision

learning_activities.topic:
The future of Australian History

In this, our last tutorial for the semester, we will think back over the course and review some of the material we have covered. We will also consider what we can make of the Australian story to 2026, how awareness of it might shape the way we think about our possible futures, and discuss the future of Australian history itself. Has History lost its relevance in the immediate term? Do we in Australia need to concentrate on more clearly vocational educational priorities? Some appear to think so, but others bemoan the attacks on History and the Humanities more generally and think that they are as important now as ever, if not more.

As part of your preparation for the week, please write a short paragraph reflecting on your experience as a student of Australian History. You might like to consider if/how your understanding of the Australian past has changed, or if anything in particular surprised or challenged you. What do you think about the importance (or not) of learning Australian History?

Readings:

1) Crotty, Martin. "The Slow Death of Academic History?: History in the Academy Since Dawkins" in The Australian History Industry eds. Paul Ashton and Paula Hamilton. Melbourne: Australian Scholarly Publishing, 2022, pp. 19-33.

2) Davison, Graeme. “Writing the History of the Future” in Lessons from History: Leading Historians Tackle Australia’s Greatest Challenges. Sydney: NewSouth, 2022, pp. 10-24

3) Nicole Townsend, “History Matters: Australian Attitudes to the Past”, War Studies Research Group, UNSW Canberra, 2026

Discussion Questions:

How does Crotty explain the decline in History departments at Australian universities over the past 30 years? Can, as Davison asks, the techniques that explain events in the past be applied to planning the future? Or is the historian’s role more to ‘educate the decision makers to think historically’ than to participate in making the decisions? What do you make of the UNSW Report’s findings? What do they suggest about broader understandings of the nature and practice of History in Australia?

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05, L06
